reasoning: Keir Starmer recently secured a massive majority in the July 2024 general election, granting him robust control over Parliament and significantly reducing the risk of a Conservative resurgence or internal revolt in the near-term. Given his commanding 174-seat majority and the typical tenure of a newly elected UK Prime Minister with such strong backing (e.g., Tony Blair, Margaret Thatcher), it is exceedingly rare for a leader to be ousted in their first full year. The grounds for departure by the end of 2025 would likely be restricted to unpredictable black swan events, severe sudden illness, or an unprecedented scandal. Though his personal poll ratings have dipped, popularity drops do not spontaneously end premierships when insulated by such a firm parliamentary majority. Assessing actuarial risks for a 62-year-old alongside a fractional chance of catastrophic scandal, the combined likelihood of Starmer resigning, dying, or being removed before December 31, 2025, remains very low, estimated around 4%.
